query: SYNTAX score predictive value for in-hospital mortality and complications in ST elevation myocardial infarction patients after primary percutaneous coronary intervention

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Coronary Artery Lesions -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Complex coronary lesions carry higher overall mortality in patients with stable angina than those with noncomplex lesions. These complex lesions are often associated with diabetes, lower ventricular ejection fraction, and lower HDL-C. The SYNTAX Score and SYNTAX Score II are frequently the means to decide on the best revascularization approach. These scoring systems are widely used and have been validated in many studies. [52] [53] [54] Studies have shown SYNTAX Score II to be superior to the SYNTAX score as a prognostic tool.

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Coronary Intervention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Comparing the outcomes in different trials for PCI vs. CABG and medical therapy, the following are the preferred treatments for different patient categories. SYNTAX score is a tool used to estimate the complexity of coronary lesions and helps determine the decision-making process between PCI or CABG. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The TIMI score ranges from 0 to 14, with each point representing a different level of risk based on the patient’s condition. [58] This scoring system helps predict mortality within the first 30 days following STEMI and is reliable for forecasting outcomes in patients receiving fibrinolytic therapy. [59] Studies have applied the TIMI score to estimate mortality rates in anterior wall STEMI cases. The TIMI score can help classify patients based on risk categories. Risk levels are categorized as low for scores ranging from 0 to 4, medium for scores between 5 and 9, and high for scores above 9. Mortality rates at 30 days for patients presenting with STEMI are between 2.5% and 10%. [60] [61] [62]

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombolysis In Myocardial Infarction Risk Score -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The thrombolysis in myocardial infarction (TIMI) score is considered a tool for early risk stratification. According to several trials, notably TIMI 11B and ESSENCE, seven factors help assess the mortality risk and risk of other adverse cardiac events, as listed below. [1] Age of 65 years or older Presence of at least three risk factors for coronary artery disease (i.e., diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, smoking, family history) Previous history of coronary stenosis of 50% or more Presence of greater than or equal to 2 episodes of angina 24 hours before the presentation Aspirin use in the past seven days ST-segment deviations greater than or equal to 0.05 mV on initial ECG at admission Elevated serum cardiac markers of necrosis

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Coronary Intervention -- Clinical Significance -- PCI vs. CABG
retrieved_docs.content: CABG is preferred in patients with two or three-vessel disease with high SYNTAX scores. [17] CABG is also preferred in patients with the left main disease or one or two-vessel disease and low SYNTAX score. CABG has shown better outcomes with multivessel disease in patients with diabetes compared with PCI. [18] [19] [20] LIMA to LAD graft has better survival irrespective of other grafts present. [21] Studies have shown better patency of internal mammary graft compared with SVG. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The Thrombolysis in Myocardial Infarction (TIMI) risk score is a straightforward tool that uses 8 prognostic factors to evaluate the risk associated with STEMI at the bedside. The TIMI risk score is the most commonly used scoring system for 30-day mortality. [57] This scoring system incorporates: Age older than 75 is assigned 3 points. Age between 64 and 74 is assigned 2 points. Diabetes, hypertension, or a history of angina is assigned 1 point. A systolic blood pressure less than 100 mm Hg is assigned 3 points. A heart rate greater than 100 beats per minute is assigned 2 points. Killip class II to IV is assigned 2 points. A body weight less than 150 lbs is assigned 1 point.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Surgery -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In the years that followed, over 25 studies have compared each successive generation of PCI (angioplasty, bare-metal stent, or drug-eluting stent) to CABG, with the overall data supporting the ACC/AHA consensus indications listed above. Discussion of the BARI, CABRI, ARTS, LE MANS, multiple MASS, multiple ERACI, multiple GABI, AWESOME, PRECOMBAT, SOS, and CARDia trials are beyond the scope of this article, but two trials FREEOM and SYNTAX may be worth summarizing. The FREEDOM trial randomized 1900 diabetic patients with multi-vessel CAD across 140 centers to treatment with either drug-eluting stent or CABG and observed a primary composite outcome of death heart attack and stroke. At five years follow up, the patients receiving PCI had higher composite death, heart attack, and stroke than patients treated with CABG (27% vs. 19% p=0.005), although CABG was associated with a higher risk of stroke. [27] The FREEDOM trial has led to the recommendation that diabetic patients who require revascularization to merit strong consideration for CABG. The SYNTAX trial randomized 1800 patients with triple vessel or left main CAD across 85 centers to treatment with either drug-eluting stent or CABG to observe a primary endpoint of death, heart attack, stroke, or need for repeat revascularization. At twelve months, largely because of a higher need for repeat revascularization in PCI, fewer CABG patients reached the primary endpoint (12% vs. 18% p = 0.002). The SYNTAX trial solidified CABG as the gold-standard treatment for left main and triple vessel disease. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Anticoagulant agents for consideration in patients with STEMI undergoing primary PCI are unfractionated heparin (UFH), low molecular weight heparin (LMWH), and bivalirudin. [7] Early evidence for the efficacy of UFH in patients with STEMI comes from the pre-fibrinolytic era. With the introduction of fibrinolysis, the publication of ISIS-2, and subsequent introduction of aspirin, the risk and benefit from the addition of UFH to fibrinolytic agents changed, but most of the evidence points out to small, but the significant net benefit of UFH when combined with aspirin versus aspirin alone in patients treated with fibrinolysis. [12] The well-known, small, randomized trial that compared UFH to enoxaparin was the ATOLL trial. [35] Around 900 STEMI patients, who received therapy with aspirin, clopidogrel, and Gp IIb/IIIAa inhibitors (in about 80% of patients) were randomized to UFH versus enoxaparin before primary PCI, and the primary outcome of death, MI complications, procedure failure, or major bleeding showed a non-significant reduction in the enoxaparin group. Data from this trial cannot be extrapolated to patients not treated with GpIIb/IIIa inhibitors and patients treated with ticagrelor or prasugrel. A meta-analysis that took place in 2012, including a large number of trials, demonstrated that enoxaparin was superior to UFH, although the study had limitations due to the inclusion of data from non-randomized trials too. A meta-analysis by Hai-Long et al. in 2018 [36] [37] demonstrated a reduced incidence of MI and death in patients with STEMI treated with enoxaparin compared to UFH, without a difference in major bleeding. At present, the latest European Society guidelines for STEMI recommend UFH as the preferred agent, but enoxaparin remains a reasonably safe and alternate consideration. The studies which evaluated bivalirudin use in patients with STEMI undergoing PCI demonstrated a higher incidence of stent thrombosis. [38] A subgroup of large observational SWEDEHEART study included STEMI patients treated with UFH compared to bivalirudin and demonstrated similar rates of early stent thrombosis, while patients treated with UFH had increased mortality. [39] Due to limited evidence for routine use of bivalirudin, currently, it remains a safe option to use bivalirudin in patients with heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.

retrieved_docs.title: Multivessel Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The SYNTAX trial compared patients who underwent CABG versus PCI with DES placement and followed-up patients in 1-year, 3 years, 5 years, and 10-year intervals. At 10 years follow-up, patients with triple vessel disease who underwent PCI with drug-eluting stents placement had a hazard ratio for death of 1.41 (CI 95%: 1.1-1.8) compared to CABG. [16] This trial had several limitations, however, the most notable of which was the utilization of first-generation paclitaxel-eluting stents and bare-metal stents. Newer generation everolimus stents have reduced risk of death, myocardial infarction, and stent thrombosis compared to earlier generations. An observational study in patients with multivessel disease compared patients who underwent PCI with everolimus stent placement versus CABG and found a similar risk of death at 2.9 years between the 2 groups with a hazard ratio of 1.04 for PCI with stent placement. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Surgery -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: More recent studies have begun to reexamine revascularization compared to medical therapy, given medical advances since the 1970s. The BARI 2D trial examined CABG, PCI, and medical therapy and found at five years for diabetic CAD patients with middle to high-risk SYNTAX score >22 (scoring system based on the angiographic appearance of coronary vessels), CABG reduced major adverse cardiac events compared to medical therapy. There were no differences between PCI and medical treatment. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombolysis In Myocardial Infarction Risk Score -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Recent studies have also shown that the amplitude of ST-segment elevation or depression and the degree of cardiac biomarker elevation (troponin T/I) can both be independent factors that could point toward initiating early invasive cardiac procedures. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombolysis In Myocardial Infarction Risk Score -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Studies have also noted that of the seven factors that contribute to the TIMI risk score, history of coronary artery disease, age greater than or equal to 65, and deviations in ST-segment on initial EKG’s were more strongly associated with adverse cardiac events as compared to the other factors. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: In the absence of absolute contraindication, anticoagulation is indicated in all patients with UA/NSTEMI once the diagnosis is made. [11] [55] [71] Enoxaparin, UFH, and fondaparinux are listed as class I of recommendation for this purpose. [11] [55] [67] Enoxaparin and fondaparinux should be avoided in patients with severe renal failure. Data from the SYNERGY trial demonstrated the non-inferiority of enoxaparin when compared to UFH in terms of efficacy, with the expense of a modest increase in bleeding risk. [72] [73] On the other hand, a meta-analysis published eight years after the SYNERGY trial reported a superior effect of enoxaparin when compared to UFH in reducing mortality and bleeding outcomes during PCI in patients with ACS. [37] The benefit was observed mostly in patients with STEMI. OASIS-5 study has demonstrated non-inferiority in the efficacy of fondaparinux compared to enoxaparin, with a better bleeding risk profile. [74] An important caveat was an increased risk of catheter-related thrombosis during PCI, which was successfully mitigated by intraprocedural UFH administration. [74] [75] Based on these observations, a reasonable approach would be that in patients undergoing early invasive strategy, where PCI is expected, the anticoagulant of choice should be UFH, while enoxaparin could be an option in the absence of bleeding risk and renal failure. Fondaparinux should be considered in patients with higher bleeding risk in the absence of advanced renal failure but would mandate intraprocedural UFH administration to prevent intra-procedural catheter thrombosis. In patients managed by ischemia-guided strategy and PCI is unlikely to happen, fondaparinux offers optimal safety profile, while observations from ESSENCE and TIMI 11B trial suggest that enoxaparin in this clinical scenario should be favored when compared to UFH. [76] [77] Bivalirudin is recommended as an anticoagulant agent in patients with UA/NSTEMI who have heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. Otherwise, bivalirudin offers a questionable benefit to a very small group of patients. Those patients would have characteristics that favor a strong inclination to add GpIIb/IIIa inhibitors and, at the same time, would have to be at increased risk of bleeding. In that situation, given non-inferiority in ischemic risk and lower incidence of bleeding with bivalirudin monotherapy compared to LMWH or UFH+GpIIb/IIIa, bivalirudin would be a choice. [68] Anticoagulation in the acute phase of UA/NSTEMI treatment is discontinued after revascularization, or in the case when no revascularization is performed, discontinuation is performed after at least 48 hours. [11] [55] [71]

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombolysis In Myocardial Infarction Risk Score -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The TIMI risk score was found to be predictive of the severity of the vascular disease, making it a powerful tool to predict the potential blood vessels of coronary circulation that could be involved.

retrieved_docs.title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with STEMI who are undergoing fibrinolysis, an anticoagulant is necessary until revascularization occurs. If there is no planned revascularization, it should be administered at least 48 hours, up to 8 days. [12] [7] Trials such as ASSENT 3 and ExTRACT-TIMI 25 demonstrated the benefit of enoxaparin when compared to UFH, although the latter study showed increased non-cerebral bleeding complications in patients treated with enoxaparin. The study also included patients with creatinine clearance less than 30 mL/min and patients older than 75 with appropriate dose adjustment. [40] [41] It seems reasonable to prefer enoxaparin over UFH in patients with STEMI undergoing fibrinolysis, although guidelines suggest class I recommendation for both agents. [12] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.

retrieved_docs.content: infarction during procedure, Postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Recognizing that each of these specific infarct types shares the overarching classification of acute infarct highlights the importance of identifying the precise arterial territory or procedural circumstance involved, which guides diagnostic evaluation, therapeutic decision‑making, and prognostic assessment in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave myocardial infarction, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to mid left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of circumflex branch of left coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of distal portion of right coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of mid portion of right coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to proximal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction involving left anterior descending coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of circumflex branch of left coronary artery, acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, and acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder) all share the myocardium as their primary site of pathology. Regardless of the specific electrocardiographic pattern (Q wave, ST‑segment elevation, or non‑ST‑segment elevation) or the particular coronary artery segment that is occluded (proximal, mid, distal, left anterior descending, circumflex, or right coronary artery), the underlying tissue injury occurs within the myocardial muscle. This common anatomic focus underscores that, in clinical assessment and therapeutic planning, the detection of myocardial injury—whether by ECG changes, cardiac biomarkers, or imaging—remains central to the diagnosis and management of all forms of acute myocardial infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2089
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct is the common morphological pattern underlying a wide spectrum of acute myocardial infarction presentations. Specific entities such as Acute Q‑wave myocardial infarction, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and numerous STEMI subtypes defined by the occluded coronary segment—including distal left anterior descending (LAD) artery, proximal LAD, diagonal branch of the anterior descending artery, septal branch of the anterior descending artery, marginal branch of the right coronary artery, posterior‑lateral branch of the right coronary artery, and distal right coronary artery—are all described as having an associated acute‑infarct morphology. In addition, Acute infarct is linked to Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, Acute non‑Q‑wave infarction (widespread), Acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), and Postoperative acute myocardial infarction. This unified morphological concept emphasizes that despite diverse clinical contexts, coronary territories, and electrocardiographic patterns, these acute coronary syndromes share the fundamental tissue change of an acute infarct, which is essential for diagnosis, risk stratification, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions caused by occlusion of the distal left anterior descending coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, or the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, as well as acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery and acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, all represent distinct presentations of an acute myocardial infarction that share a common pathological substrate in the myocardium. Acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are additional variants of acute myocardial infarction, each reflecting different electrocardiographic patterns, timing, or clinical contexts, yet each also localizes its injury to the myocardial tissue. Collectively, these entities illustrate how diverse coronary artery occlusions, ECG morphologies, and clinical settings converge on myocardial damage as the central finding site, underscoring the importance of recognizing the specific type of infarction for appropriate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 976
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to these STEMI morphologies, Acute infarct also includes Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction during procedure, Acute widespread myocardial infarction, Postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Recognizing that all of these distinct anatomic and clinical variants share the overarching concept of acute infarct helps clinicians accurately classify, risk‑stratify, and manage patients presenting with acute coronary syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2007
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute Q wave myocardial infarction, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to mid left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the intermediate artery, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the mid portion of the right coronary artery, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the posterior‑lateral branch of the right coronary artery, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the septal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction, and acute widespread myocardial infarction are all conditions in which the finding site is the myocardium. This common involvement of the myocardial tissue underscores that, regardless of the specific electrocardiographic pattern (Q‑wave, non‑Q‑wave, ST‑segment elevation, or non‑ST‑segment elevation) or the particular coronary artery segment that is occluded (including the left anterior descending, intermediate, right coronary, posterior‑lateral, septal, or circumflex branches), the primary pathological substrate is injury to the heart muscle itself. Recognizing the myocardium as the shared site of injury across these diverse presentations is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and the implementation of targeted therapeutic strategies aimed at preserving myocardial function and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1153
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction (AMI) is a spectrum of coronary syndromes in which the primary site of injury is the myocardium, manifesting with a characteristic “acute infarct” morphology that includes early necrosis, inflammation and edema of cardiac myocytes. The spectrum encompasses acute Q‑wave infarction (transmural necrosis with a specific Q‑wave morphology), acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) of various electrocardiographic patterns, acute non‑Q‑wave infarction, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction. All of these clinical entities are linked to occlusion of specific coronary arterial branches, each producing a localized myocardial injury that corresponds to the territory supplied by the blocked vessel. Reported occlusion sites include the proximal, mid and distal segments of the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the left coronary artery (LCA) as a whole, the circumflex branch of the LCA, the diagonal and septal branches of the LAD, the intermediate artery, and numerous branches of the right coronary artery (RCA) such as its proximal, mid, distal, marginal, posterior descending, posterior‑lateral and septal branches. Occlusion of any of these vessels generates the hallmark ST‑segment elevation on the electrocardiogram (in STEMI) while the underlying pathology remains confined to myocardial tissue. Recognizing the precise relationship between the anatomic location of coronary blockage, the resulting electrocardiographic pattern and the uniform acute infarct histopathology is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification and timely reperfusion therapy in patients with acute coronary syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2247
retrieved_docs.content: Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to mid left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of anterior descending branch of left coronary artery, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of circumflex branch of left coronary artery, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of distal portion of right coronary artery, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of mid portion of right coronary artery, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of posterior descending branch of right coronary artery, Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of circumflex branch of left coronary artery, Acute non‑Q wave infarction, Acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, Acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), Acute widespread myocardial infarction, and Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction all share the same anatomic finding site: the myocardium (myokard). This common localization indicates that, irrespective of the specific coronary artery involved, the presence or absence of ST‑segment elevation, Q‑wave formation, or the extent of the infarction, the primary tissue affected is the myocardial muscle. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities all reflect myocardial injury guides clinicians to focus diagnostic imaging, biomarker assessment, and therapeutic strategies on preserving myocardial function and limiting infarct size.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2399
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction, in its many clinical presentations—including ST‑segment elevation infarctions caused by occlusion of the left anterior descending artery, its septal or proximal branches, the intermediate artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending or posterior lateral branches of the right coronary artery, as well as non‑ST‑segment elevation infarctions, widespread infarctions, and those occurring during procedures or postoperatively—always involves injury to the myocardium. Regardless of the specific coronary vessel or segment that is blocked, the pathological finding site for each of these myocardial infarction subtypes is the myocardial tissue itself, underscoring that the heart muscle is the central organ affected across the full spectrum of acute infarct presentations. This unified understanding emphasizes that diagnostic evaluation, therapeutic interventions, and patient monitoring should focus on the extent and location of myocardial damage, irrespective of the particular coronary artery involved or the clinical context of the infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2309
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct is associated with a broad spectrum of morphological presentations that reflect the underlying coronary pathology and clinical context. Among the Q‑wave patterns, acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread and acute Q‑wave myocardial infarction are recognized morphologies. Non‑Q‑wave presentations are also linked to acute infarct, including acute non‑Q‑wave infarction. Various forms of acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) are defined by the specific coronary artery or branch that is occluded: acute STEMI due to distal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, due to left coronary artery occlusion, due to mid left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, due to occlusion of the diagonal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, due to occlusion of the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, due to occlusion of the posterior‑lateral branch of the right coronary artery, due to occlusion of the proximal portion of the right coronary artery, and due to right coronary artery occlusion. In addition, acute infarct may occur in procedural settings, described as acute myocardial infarction during a procedure. Non‑ST‑segment elevation presentations are also captured, with acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI) and a subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction being recognized morphologies linked to the acute infarct entity. Together, these relationships illustrate how the type and location of coronary artery obstruction, as well as procedural circumstances, determine the specific morphological pattern of an acute myocardial infarction, guiding diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted patient management.

Recognizing these diverse entities under the shared morphology of Acute infarct aids clinicians in precise diagnosis, risk stratification, and selection of targeted therapeutic strategies for patients presenting with acute coronary syndromes.
